v1/plan.md.text:
when you say Singleton, I dont think that is right, it's just a single instance on the app class, being singleton it would fail with having multiple apps running in a single goal

```plang
- create app 'myApp', write to %myApp%
- create app 'myApp2', write to %myApp2%
- run %myApp%
- run %myApp%
```

this is not an option today but will be. it is just an instance on app class, no singleton
v1/plan.md.author: user

v1/plan.md.text: I have a bit of problem with Services being under each actor since the Identity is always the system identity. it feels icky, having system sign something that lives under user. Is it wrong to have it live there?
v1/plan.md.author: user

v1/plan.md.text:
Fair, the placement and identity mismatch is a real smell. Three options:

1. **Services under System only** — `app.System.Services`. Identity-location aligned (System owns what System signs). Each Service has `Parent` ref to whoever triggered it (User or System). Lose the per-actor navigation.

2. **Flat `App.Services`** — single collection on App. Each Service has `Parent`. No actor-bound location, no icky split. Simplest.

3. **Stay per-actor** (current plan) — accept the icky factor as the cost of preserving "who triggered this" via location.

My lean: 2. Flat `App.Services` cleanly separates origin (Parent) from identity (always System). The Actor-owned `Services` collection was me overfitting OBP rule 5 — the collections real owner is App, not Actor. Whats your read?
v1/plan.md.author: architect

v1/plan.md.text:
what happens when we try to write to channel that doesn't exist? like here we are creating 2 (fix that stdin, please) channels, but no error channel, so where do errors go? and I just mean in general, what should happen, just funnel them through default?

You mentioned in our chat, "input"/"output"/"error" somewhere, I think thats better than default, stdin, etc.
v1/plan.md.author: user

v1/plan.md.text:
Three things addressed:

1. **Channel name rename** — agreed. Standard names now match Role: `output`/`error`/`input` (was `default`/`error`/`stdin`). Updated everywhere.

2. **Web example missing error channel** — fixed. Web example now registers all three (output/error/input). Added a note in the example: entry points are responsible for registering all three for any actor that does I/O.

3. **What happens when a channel doesnt exist** — added a new section above this point. My take: hard typed error (`ChannelNotFound`), no silent fallback to `output`. Reasoning: silent fallback hides bugs; a typo (`to dbug`) should be loud. Same for missing standard role-channels — entry-point incompleteness should fail at use, not get swallowed. Program can catch via standard error handling if it wants to recover.

If youd rather have a fallback (e.g. `error` falls back to `output` when missing), say so — thats a real alternative; I picked strict because its safer by default but it does mean entry points must be complete.
v1/plan.md.author: architect

v1/plan.md.text: it should be app.currentUser.Services.New(); because maybe we are running as system
v1/plan.md.author: user

v1/plan.md.text:
(Reposting here — original reply was anchored at line 104, which became blank after plan edits, hence invisible.)

Three things from your line-104 comment:

1. **Channel name rename** — done. `default`/`stdin` → `output`/`input` everywhere.
2. **Web example missing error channel** — fixed. Web example now registers all three (output/error/input).
3. **Missing channel behavior** — added this section above. My take: hard typed error (`ChannelNotFound`), no silent fallback. Reasoning: silent fallback hides bugs; a typo (`to dbug`) should be loud.

Open question: do you want a fallback for the special case of `error` falling back to `output` when error is missing? Strict says no; ergonomic says maybe. I picked strict. Push back if you want fallback for error specifically.
v1/plan.md.author: architect
